name: TinyDB (Lightweight Document Database)

auth.notes: Local library; JSON file storage; no auth

performance.notes: Pure Python; reads entire table into memory; not for large datasets

agent_readiness.notes: Tiny document database, no server required. from tinydb import TinyDB, Query. db = TinyDB('db.json'). db.insert({'name': 'Alice', 'age': 30}). db.insert_multiple([{...}, {...}]). User = Query(). db.search(User.name == 'Alice'). db.search(User.age > 25). db.update({'age': 31}, User.name == 'Alice'). db.remove(User.age < 18). db.all(). db.get(User.name == 'Alice'). db.count(User.age > 25). table = db.table('users'). MemoryStorage: TinyDB(storage=MemoryStorage). CachingMiddleware for performance. Custom storage: any dict-serializable backend.

gotchas: Reads entire table into memory — not suitable for large datasets (>100K records)
No concurrent writes — single-process only; file locking not guaranteed
No SQL queries — TinyDB Query language is Python-based, not SQL
JSON-only natively — binary storage requires custom storage class
No relationships — flat document store only
best_when: Lightweight local storage for small apps, tools, and prototypes
Config/state persistence without a database server
CLI tools needing simple key-value or document storage
Testing and prototyping before migrating to SQLite or MongoDB
avoid_when: Large datasets (>10K documents) — use SQLite or MongoDB
When SQLite is available (same overhead, better durability)
summary: TinyDB is a lightweight Python document database (JSON file backend). TinyDB('db.json').insert(dict). Query(): db.search(User.age > 25). update(), remove(), all(). MemoryStorage for in-memory. MIT by Markus Siemens. Type-annotated. Best for small local storage in tools and prototypes. Reads entire table into memory. Single-process only. No relationships. Use SQLite for anything beyond a few thousand documents.
